splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability from multiemployer pension plans to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year, or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be selected by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the supervision period beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled while a supervisee is a fugitive, meaning the clock stops and the court retains jurisdiction to revoke supervised release beyond the original end date.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to extend the period of supervision beyond what is specified in the judgment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether the gatekeeping provision of 28 U.S.C. § 2244(b)(1), which categorically bars habeas petitioners from raising claims previously presented, applies to federal prisoners seeking successive collateral attacks under § 2255. Six circuits apply the bar while three do not, creating a significant disparity in access to post-conviction relief for federal prisoners.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar on previously raised claims applies to successive § 2255 motions, imposing a stricter gatekeeping requirement on federal prisoners in addition to § 2255(h)'s requirements.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, meaning they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to be met for disqualification) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief under the First Step Act only if they meet all three listed criminal-history criteria, or whether possessing any one is disqualifying. This directly affects whether district courts must impose sentences without regard to statutory minimums, with significant liberty implications.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive or distributive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as disjunctive or distributive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps agency/Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the rule of lenity should prevail over deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations of ambiguous criminal provisions. At least three circuits hold lenity trumps deference, while the Third Circuit has held that agency deference trumps lenity. The issue has become more significant after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over Auer/Chevron-type deference to agency interpretations in criminal contexts, so ambiguous criminal provisions must be construed in the defendant's favor rather than deferring to the government's reading.

positions.label: Agency deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that deference to the Sentencing Commission's or agency's interpretation of ambiguous guidelines or criminal provisions takes precedence over the rule of lenity.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines commentary (Application Note 1) may expand the definition of 'controlled substance offense' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy when the guideline text does not mention them.
splits.description: Circuits have split on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds inchoate offenses (conspiracy, aiding and abetting, attempt) to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation entitled to deference under Stinson, or instead impermissibly expands the guideline's unambiguous text. This affects career offender designations and sentencing ranges for many federal defendants.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and that inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as 'controlled substance offenses' under § 4B1.2(b) based on deference to the Sentencing Commission.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and that Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition beyond its plain meaning, so conspiracy does not qualify as a 'controlled substance offense.'

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule covers substances not listed in the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the career offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally controlled, or whether any state-law drug offense qualifies regardless of federal scheduling. This significantly affects Guidelines calculations and sentencing ranges for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State offenses qualify regardless of federal scheduling
positions.summary: Holds that there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the career offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense,' so state drug offenses qualify even if the substance is not federally scheduled.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), may be considered as a factor supporting 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive statutory changes — particularly the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory consecutive 'stacked' § 924(c) sentences — as contributing to extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically prohibit reliance on such changes, while others allow them as one factor among many.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A), whether alone or in combination with other factors.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered as a factor
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's property belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between a Chapter 13 filing and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 becomes part of the bankruptcy estate available to creditors, or remains with the debtor. This question significantly affects debtors who experience changes in property values during their Chapter 13 cases and then convert to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity inure to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion, based on the broad scope of 'property of the estate' under § 541(a) and the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, consistent with the statute's structure, object, and legislative history.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an appeal from the denial of a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits have split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to the civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case (subject to the criminal appeal deadline). The Seventh Circuit has joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the civil appeal deadline (30 days under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a)) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal case, so the criminal appeal deadline (14 days under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b)) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants at supervised release revocation proceedings for the conduct that violated the conditions of supervision, or whether revocation sanctions are limited to addressing the breach of trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether revocation of supervised release may be based on punishment for the underlying violation conduct or must be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust inherent in violating release conditions. The Sixth Circuit has allowed express punishment for violation conduct, placing it at the extreme end of a circuit split.

positions.label: Courts may punish for violation conduct
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly consider and punish defendants for the conduct underlying the supervised release violation at revocation proceedings.

positions.label: Revocation sanctions address breach of trust only
positions.summary: Holds that sanctions upon revocation of supervised release should be calibrated to the breach of the court's trust inherent in the violation, not to independently punish the defendant for the underlying conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as effectively unreviewable on appeal from final judgment.
splits.description: Circuits have not uniformly addressed whether denial of a motion to seal medical records qualifies as a collateral order under Cohen v. Beneficial Industrial Loan Corp. The D.C. Circuit held that medical privacy is a 'value of high order' warranting immediate appellate review, recognizing that once medical information is publicly disclosed, the harm cannot be remedied on post-judgment appeal. Other circuits have not addressed this specific question or have applied different frameworks.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' that warrants immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine because delayed review risks irreversible disclosure.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that sealing orders generally do not qualify for immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine, as parties may seek post-judgment review or other procedural protections.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: In re City of New York

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption provision, which prevents states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier,' extends to preempt state common-law negligence claims brought against freight brokers for injuries caused by carrier operations. This affects liability exposure for the freight brokerage industry and compensation for accident victims.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's broad preemption clause extends to state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims relate to the broker's services in arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA does not preempt state-law negligence claims against freight brokers, as such common-law tort claims are not the type of state regulation that Congress intended to preempt.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances—particularly marijuana—survives Second Amendment scrutiny under the historical tradition test. This affects millions of marijuana users in states where marijuana has been legalized under state law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that the historical tradition of disarming individuals who pose a danger to society supports the constitutionality of § 922(g)(3) as applied to marijuana users.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because there is no sufficient historical analogue supporting the disarmament of persons who use controlled substances.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether arguments that could be raised in a habeas petition under § 2255 — such as claims of legal error in the original conviction or sentence — may also be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons supporting compassionate release. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors while others hold they are exclusively cognizable under § 2255.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claims of legal error or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that claims challenging the validity of a conviction or sentence are not cognizable as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release and must be brought exclusively through § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to state voting rules, including ballot-counting deadlines. The resolution affects how easily pre-election challenges can be brought to voting procedures nationwide and whether such disputes are funneled into post-election litigation.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate has Article III standing to challenge election rules that allegedly affect the validity or counting of ballots in their race, recognizing competitive injury as sufficient.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's generalized competitive interest is insufficient to establish Article III standing to challenge state ballot-counting procedures before an election.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bognet v. Secretary of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
